questTitle: O-Level Chemistry: Chemical Bonding - Advanced Applications Worksheet 2
subject: Chemistry
description: Deepen your understanding of chemical bonding! This worksheet focuses on comparing ionic and covalent compounds, understanding polyatomic ions, explaining properties based on structure and bonding, and applying knowledge to unfamiliar contexts. Show clear reasoning in your answers!

questions.question: Which statement correctly compares ionic and covalent bonding?
options: Ionic bonding involves sharing electrons; covalent bonding involves transfer of electrons
Ionic bonding involves transfer of electrons and forms between metals and non-metals; covalent bonding involves sharing electrons and forms between non-metals
Both involve sharing of electrons but ionic bonds are stronger
Both involve transfer of electrons but covalent bonds form ions
questions.answer: Ionic bonding involves transfer of electrons and forms between metals and non-metals; covalent bonding involves sharing electrons and forms between non-metals

questions.question: In a sample of solid iodine (I₂), what forces must be overcome when it sublimes (changes from solid to gas)?
options: Strong covalent bonds within iodine molecules
Weak intermolecular forces of attraction between iodine molecules
Strong ionic bonds between iodine ions
Strong electrostatic forces between I⁺ and I⁻ ions
questions.answer: Weak intermolecular forces of attraction between iodine molecules

questions.question: When sodium chloride is heated until it melts, what is overcome?
options: Weak intermolecular forces between NaCl molecules
Covalent bonds between Na and Cl atoms
Strong electrostatic forces of attraction between Na⁺ and Cl⁻ ions
Weak ionic bonds in the lattice
questions.answer: Strong electrostatic forces of attraction between Na⁺ and Cl⁻ ions

questions.question: Predict the type of bonding in potassium bromide (KBr).
options: Covalent bonding (K and Br are both non-metals)
Ionic bonding (K is a metal and Br is a non-metal)
Metallic bonding
No bonding occurs between K and Br
questions.answer: Ionic bonding (K is a metal and Br is a non-metal)

questions.question: Predict the type of bonding in phosphorus trichloride (PCl₃).
options: Ionic bonding (P transfers electrons to Cl)
Metallic bonding
Covalent bonding (P and Cl are both non-metals)
Both ionic and covalent bonding
questions.answer: Covalent bonding (P and Cl are both non-metals)

questions.question: Predict the type of bonding in calcium nitrate (Ca(NO₃)₂).
options: Only ionic bonding
Only covalent bonding
Ionic bonding between Ca²⁺ and NO₃⁻ ions; covalent bonding within the NO₃⁻ ion
No bonding - it is a mixture
questions.answer: Ionic bonding between Ca²⁺ and NO₃⁻ ions; covalent bonding within the NO₃⁻ ion

questions.question:
Identify the type(s) of bonding present in each compound:

(a) MgO
(b) H₂S
(c) (NH₄)₂SO₄
options: (a) Covalent; (b) Ionic; (c) Ionic only
(a) Ionic; (b) Covalent; (c) Covalent only
(a) Ionic; (b) Covalent; (c) Both ionic and covalent
(a) Covalent; (b) Covalent; (c) Both ionic and covalent
questions.answer: (a) Ionic; (b) Covalent; (c) Both ionic and covalent

questions.question: Explain why magnesium oxide (MgO, melting point 2852°C) has a much higher melting point than sodium chloride (NaCl, melting point 801°C).
options: MgO has covalent bonds while NaCl has ionic bonds
MgO has weaker electrostatic forces than NaCl
Mg²⁺ and O²⁻ have higher charges (2+ and 2-) than Na⁺ and Cl⁻ (1+ and 1-), resulting in stronger electrostatic forces of attraction
MgO has smaller ions so the forces are weaker
questions.answer: Mg²⁺ and O²⁻ have higher charges (2+ and 2-) than Na⁺ and Cl⁻ (1+ and 1-), resulting in stronger electrostatic forces of attraction

questions.question: Bromine (Br₂) has a boiling point of 59°C while potassium bromide (KBr) has a melting point of 734°C. Explain this difference.
options: Br₂ has stronger bonds than KBr
Br₂ is a simple covalent molecule with weak intermolecular forces; KBr is an ionic compound with strong electrostatic forces between K⁺ and Br⁻ ions
KBr has weak ionic bonds; Br₂ has strong covalent bonds
Both are ionic but Br₂ has fewer ions
questions.answer: Br₂ is a simple covalent molecule with weak intermolecular forces; KBr is an ionic compound with strong electrostatic forces between K⁺ and Br⁻ ions

questions.question: Hydrogen fluoride (HF) has a higher boiling point than hydrogen chloride (HCl), even though both are simple covalent molecules. Why might this be?
options: HF has stronger covalent bonds within the molecule
HF has stronger intermolecular forces between molecules than HCl
HF is ionic while HCl is covalent
HCl has more electrons so should have a higher boiling point
questions.answer: HF has stronger intermolecular forces between molecules than HCl

questions.question:
Arrange the following in order of increasing melting point and explain:

A: Oxygen (O₂)
B: Sodium oxide (Na₂O)
C: Sulfur dioxide (SO₂)
options: B < C < A; all are covalent with increasing molecular size
A < C < B; A and C are simple covalent with weak intermolecular forces, B is ionic with strong electrostatic forces
C < A < B; only B has weak intermolecular forces
A < B < C; B is covalent, C is ionic
questions.answer: A < C < B; A and C are simple covalent with weak intermolecular forces, B is ionic with strong electrostatic forces

questions.question: Solid lead(II) bromide does not conduct electricity, but molten lead(II) bromide does. What happens to the ions when lead(II) bromide melts?
options: New ions are created when it melts
The ions, which were in fixed positions in the solid lattice, become free to move
Free electrons are released from the lead atoms
The covalent bonds break to form ions
questions.answer: The ions, which were in fixed positions in the solid lattice, become free to move

questions.question: Ethanol (C₂H₅OH) does not conduct electricity even when liquid. Why?
options: Ethanol is a solid at room temperature
Ethanol is a simple covalent compound with no ions or free electrons to carry charge
Ethanol is an ionic compound but ions cannot move
Ethanol molecules are too large to conduct
questions.answer: Ethanol is a simple covalent compound with no ions or free electrons to carry charge

questions.question: Hydrochloric acid (HCl dissolved in water) conducts electricity, but hydrogen chloride gas does not. Explain why.
options: HCl gas has ions but they cannot move in the gas state
When HCl dissolves in water, it ionises to form H⁺ and Cl⁻ ions which are free to move; HCl gas consists of neutral covalent molecules with no ions
HCl gas conducts better than the solution
Water provides the electrons needed for conduction
questions.answer: When HCl dissolves in water, it ionises to form H⁺ and Cl⁻ ions which are free to move; HCl gas consists of neutral covalent molecules with no ions

questions.question: Glucose (C₆H₁₂O₆) dissolves in water but the solution does not conduct electricity. Sodium chloride dissolves in water and the solution conducts electricity. Explain the difference.
options: Glucose is insoluble so cannot conduct
Glucose is a simple covalent compound that remains as neutral molecules when dissolved; NaCl is ionic and dissociates into free-moving ions when dissolved
Both dissociate into ions but glucose ions are larger
Sodium chloride has more electrons available for conduction
questions.answer: Glucose is a simple covalent compound that remains as neutral molecules when dissolved; NaCl is ionic and dissociates into free-moving ions when dissolved

questions.question: Predict and explain whether molten sodium hydroxide (NaOH) would conduct electricity.
options: No, because NaOH is a covalent compound
Yes, because when molten, Na⁺ and OH⁻ ions are free to move and carry charge
No, because the OH⁻ ion is covalent so NaOH cannot conduct
Yes, because electrons are released when NaOH melts
questions.answer: Yes, because when molten, Na⁺ and OH⁻ ions are free to move and carry charge

questions.question: The hydroxide ion (OH⁻) contains one oxygen atom and one hydrogen atom. What type of bonding exists between O and H in this ion?
options: Ionic bonding
Covalent bonding
Metallic bonding
No bonding - they are separate ions
questions.answer: Covalent bonding

questions.question: The carbonate ion (CO₃²⁻) has a charge of 2-. What type of bonding exists between the carbon and oxygen atoms within this ion?
options: Ionic bonding with 2- charge distributed among atoms
Covalent bonding between the carbon and oxygen atoms
Metallic bonding
Electrostatic forces between C²⁺ and O⁻ ions
questions.answer: Covalent bonding between the carbon and oxygen atoms

questions.question: In calcium carbonate (CaCO₃), identify all types of bonding present.
options: Only ionic bonding between Ca, C, and O
Only covalent bonding throughout
Covalent bonding within the CO₃²⁻ ion; ionic bonding (strong electrostatic forces) between Ca²⁺ and CO₃²⁻ ions
Weak intermolecular forces between Ca and CO₃
questions.answer: Covalent bonding within the CO₃²⁻ ion; ionic bonding (strong electrostatic forces) between Ca²⁺ and CO₃²⁻ ions

questions.question: Ammonium phosphate has the formula (NH₄)₃PO₄. Identify all types of bonding present.
options: Only ionic bonding
Only covalent bonding
Covalent bonding within NH₄⁺ ions, covalent bonding within PO₄³⁻ ions, and ionic bonding between NH₄⁺ and PO₄³⁻ ions
Ionic bonding within NH₄⁺ and PO₄³⁻, covalent bonding between them
questions.answer: Covalent bonding within NH₄⁺ ions, covalent bonding within PO₄³⁻ ions, and ionic bonding between NH₄⁺ and PO₄³⁻ ions

questions.question:
When sodium sulfate (Na₂SO₄) is melted and then electrolysed, what happens at the electrodes?

Consider the types of bonding and which bonds are broken.
options: The covalent bonds within SO₄²⁻ break first
Na⁺ ions move to the cathode and are reduced; SO₄²⁻ or its decomposition products move to the anode - the ionic bonds are overcome, not the covalent bonds within SO₄²⁻
No electrolysis occurs because SO₄²⁻ is covalent
The SO₄²⁻ ion moves to the cathode
questions.answer: Na⁺ ions move to the cathode and are reduced; SO₄²⁻ or its decomposition products move to the anode - the ionic bonds are overcome, not the covalent bonds within SO₄²⁻

questions.problemSet: TOPIC 5: Comparing Compounds with Polyatomic Ions
questions.question: Compare sodium sulfate (Na₂SO₄) and sulfur trioxide (SO₃). Which has the higher melting point and why?
options: SO₃ has higher melting point because S-O covalent bonds are strong
Na₂SO₄ has higher melting point because it is ionic with strong electrostatic forces; SO₃ is a simple covalent molecule with weak intermolecular forces
Both have similar melting points because both contain sulfur and oxygen
SO₃ has higher melting point because it has more atoms
questions.answer: Na₂SO₄ has higher melting point because it is ionic with strong electrostatic forces; SO₃ is a simple covalent molecule with weak intermolecular forces

questions.problemSet: TOPIC 5: Comparing Compounds with Polyatomic Ions
questions.question: Both sodium nitrate (NaNO₃) and nitrogen dioxide (NO₂) contain nitrogen and oxygen. Compare their electrical conductivity.
options: Both conduct electricity when molten
Neither conducts electricity in any state
NaNO₃ conducts when molten or dissolved (ionic, free-moving ions); NO₂ does not conduct in any state (simple covalent, no ions)
NO₂ conducts better because it has more oxygen atoms
questions.answer: NaNO₃ conducts when molten or dissolved (ionic, free-moving ions); NO₂ does not conduct in any state (simple covalent, no ions)

questions.problemSet: TOPIC 5: Comparing Compounds with Polyatomic Ions
questions.question: Ammonium chloride (NH₄Cl) and methane (CH₄) both contain nitrogen/carbon bonded to hydrogen atoms. Compare their properties.
options: Both are simple covalent compounds with low melting points
Both are ionic compounds with high melting points
NH₄Cl is ionic with high melting point and conducts when molten; CH₄ is simple covalent with very low melting point and does not conduct
CH₄ is ionic; NH₄Cl is covalent
questions.answer: NH₄Cl is ionic with high melting point and conducts when molten; CH₄ is simple covalent with very low melting point and does not conduct

questions.problemSet: TOPIC 5: Comparing Compounds with Polyatomic Ions ⭐
questions.question:
Compare the bonding and properties of:

A: CO₂ (carbon dioxide)
B: Na₂CO₃ (sodium carbonate)

Both contain carbon and oxygen. What are the key differences?
options: Both are ionic with similar properties
Both are covalent with low melting points
CO₂ is simple covalent (low MP, doesn't conduct); Na₂CO₃ is ionic containing the covalent CO₃²⁻ ion (high MP, conducts when molten/dissolved)
CO₂ is ionic; Na₂CO₃ is covalent
questions.answer: CO₂ is simple covalent (low MP, doesn't conduct); Na₂CO₃ is ionic containing the covalent CO₃²⁻ ion (high MP, conducts when molten/dissolved)

questions.question:
Rank the following forces from weakest to strongest:

A: Intermolecular forces between CO₂ molecules
B: Covalent bonds within a CO₂ molecule
C: Electrostatic forces between Na⁺ and Cl⁻ ions in NaCl
options: B < A < C
C < B < A
A < C < B
A < B ≈ C (intermolecular forces are weakest; covalent bonds and ionic bonds are both strong)
questions.answer: A < B ≈ C (intermolecular forces are weakest; covalent bonds and ionic bonds are both strong)

questions.question: When comparing ionic compounds, which factors affect the strength of the electrostatic forces of attraction between ions?
options: Only the size of the ions
Only the charge on the ions
Both the charge on the ions and the size of the ions
The number of electrons in the outer shell only
questions.answer: Both the charge on the ions and the size of the ions

questions.question: Lithium fluoride (LiF) and caesium iodide (CsI) are both ionic compounds. LiF has a much higher melting point. Why?
options: LiF has weaker ionic bonds
Li⁺ and F⁻ are smaller ions than Cs⁺ and I⁻, so the ions are closer together and the electrostatic forces of attraction are stronger
CsI has covalent character
LiF has more ions in its lattice
questions.answer: Li⁺ and F⁻ are smaller ions than Cs⁺ and I⁻, so the ions are closer together and the electrostatic forces of attraction are stronger

questions.question:
Arrange these ionic compounds in order of increasing melting point and justify:

A: NaCl (Na⁺ and Cl⁻)
B: MgO (Mg²⁺ and O²⁻)
C: KBr (K⁺ and Br⁻)
options: B < A < C; MgO has smallest ions
A < B < C; alphabetical order
C < A < B; KBr has largest ions with 1+ and 1- charges (weakest), NaCl has medium-sized ions with 1+ and 1- charges, MgO has small ions with 2+ and 2- charges (strongest)
B < C < A; MgO is covalent
questions.answer: C < A < B; KBr has largest ions with 1+ and 1- charges (weakest), NaCl has medium-sized ions with 1+ and 1- charges, MgO has small ions with 2+ and 2- charges (strongest)

questions.question:
A student heats four substances and records the temperatures at which they melt:

P: 45°C
Q: 770°C
R: -78°C
S: 1610°C

Which substances are most likely ionic compounds?
options: P and R
Q and S
P and Q
R and S
questions.answer: Q and S

questions.question:
Substance X has the following properties:
- Melting point: 25°C
- Does not conduct electricity as solid or liquid
- Low solubility in water

What type of structure does X have?
options: Giant ionic lattice
Simple molecular (covalent)
Metallic structure
Ionic compound with polyatomic ions
questions.answer: Simple molecular (covalent)

questions.question:
Substance Y has the following properties:
- High melting point (over 800°C)
- Does not conduct electricity as a solid
- Conducts electricity when dissolved in water
- Soluble in water

What type of structure does Y have?
options: Simple molecular (covalent)
Giant ionic lattice
Metallic structure
Simple atomic structure
questions.answer: Giant ionic lattice

questions.question: When solid potassium hydroxide (KOH) is added to water, the solution conducts electricity. Explain why.
options: KOH molecules carry the current
Water molecules become charged when KOH is added
KOH is an ionic compound that dissociates in water to form K⁺ and OH⁻ ions that are free to move and carry charge
Electrons are released from the potassium atoms
questions.answer: KOH is an ionic compound that dissociates in water to form K⁺ and OH⁻ ions that are free to move and carry charge

questions.question: Silicon tetrachloride (SiCl₄) is a liquid at room temperature that does not conduct electricity and has a low boiling point. Sodium chloride (NaCl) is a solid at room temperature that conducts electricity when molten. Both contain chlorine. Explain the difference in properties.
options: Both are ionic but SiCl₄ has weaker ionic bonds
SiCl₄ is a simple covalent molecule with weak intermolecular forces; NaCl is an ionic compound with strong electrostatic forces between ions
SiCl₄ has more chlorine atoms so has weaker bonds
NaCl is covalent with strong bonds; SiCl₄ is ionic with weak bonds
questions.answer: SiCl₄ is a simple covalent molecule with weak intermolecular forces; NaCl is an ionic compound with strong electrostatic forces between ions

questions.question: A compound has the formula XY₄ where X is in Group 4 and Y is in Group 7. Would you expect this compound to be ionic or covalent? Explain.
options: Ionic, because Y atoms will gain electrons from X
Covalent, because both X (Group 4) and Y (Group 7) are non-metals, so electrons will be shared
Ionic, because all compounds with 4 Y atoms are ionic
Cannot be determined from the information given
questions.answer: Covalent, because both X (Group 4) and Y (Group 7) are non-metals, so electrons will be shared

questions.question:
A student states: 'Simple covalent compounds have low melting points because covalent bonds are weak.'

Is this statement correct?
options: Yes, covalent bonds are weak
No. Covalent bonds are strong, but simple covalent compounds have low melting points because the intermolecular forces between molecules are weak
Yes, all bonds in covalent compounds are weak
No, simple covalent compounds have high melting points
questions.answer: No. Covalent bonds are strong, but simple covalent compounds have low melting points because the intermolecular forces between molecules are weak

questions.question:
A student states: 'Sodium chloride conducts electricity because it contains electrons.'

Is this statement correct?
options: Yes, electrons carry the current in NaCl
No. NaCl does conduct (when molten or dissolved), but the current is carried by mobile ions (Na⁺ and Cl⁻), not electrons
Yes, NaCl releases free electrons when it melts
No, NaCl never conducts electricity
questions.answer: No. NaCl does conduct (when molten or dissolved), but the current is carried by mobile ions (Na⁺ and Cl⁻), not electrons

questions.question:
A student states: 'In ammonium chloride (NH₄Cl), the bonding between nitrogen and hydrogen is ionic because NH₄Cl is an ionic compound.'

Is this statement correct?
options: Yes, all bonding in ionic compounds is ionic
No. While NH₄Cl is an ionic compound (containing NH₄⁺ and Cl⁻), the bonding within the NH₄⁺ ion between N and H is covalent
Yes, nitrogen transfers electrons to hydrogen
No, NH₄Cl is not an ionic compound
questions.answer: No. While NH₄Cl is an ionic compound (containing NH₄⁺ and Cl⁻), the bonding within the NH₄⁺ ion between N and H is covalent

questions.question:
A student states: 'When ice melts, the covalent bonds between hydrogen and oxygen are broken.'

Identify and correct the error.
options: The statement is correct
Error: When ice melts, the weak intermolecular forces between water molecules are overcome, NOT the covalent bonds within water molecules. The covalent bonds remain intact.
Error: Ice does not contain covalent bonds
Error: Ice does not melt, it sublimes
questions.answer: Error: When ice melts, the weak intermolecular forces between water molecules are overcome, NOT the covalent bonds within water molecules. The covalent bonds remain intact.

questions.question:
Complete the table:

| Property | Ionic Compounds | Simple Covalent Compounds |
|----------|-----------------|---------------------------|
| Melting point | ? | ? |
options: Ionic: Low; Simple Covalent: High
Ionic: High; Simple Covalent: High
Ionic: Low; Simple Covalent: Low
Ionic: High (strong electrostatic forces); Simple Covalent: Low (weak intermolecular forces)
questions.answer: Ionic: High (strong electrostatic forces); Simple Covalent: Low (weak intermolecular forces)

questions.question:
Complete the table:

| Property | Ionic Compounds | Simple Covalent Compounds |
|----------|-----------------|---------------------------|
| Electrical conductivity (solid) | ? | ? |
options: Ionic: Conducts; Simple Covalent: Conducts
Ionic: Does not conduct (ions fixed); Simple Covalent: Does not conduct (no ions/free electrons)
Ionic: Does not conduct; Simple Covalent: Conducts
Ionic: Conducts; Simple Covalent: Does not conduct
questions.answer: Ionic: Does not conduct (ions fixed); Simple Covalent: Does not conduct (no ions/free electrons)

questions.question:
Complete the table:

| Property | Ionic Compounds | Simple Covalent Compounds |
|----------|-----------------|---------------------------|
| Electrical conductivity (molten) | ? | ? |
options: Ionic: Does not conduct; Simple Covalent: Conducts
Ionic: Conducts (mobile ions); Simple Covalent: Does not conduct (still no ions/free electrons)
Both conduct when molten
Neither conducts when molten
questions.answer: Ionic: Conducts (mobile ions); Simple Covalent: Does not conduct (still no ions/free electrons)

questions.question: What is the main structural difference between ionic compounds and simple covalent compounds?
options: Ionic compounds are made of molecules; simple covalent compounds are made of ions
Ionic compounds form giant lattices of ions; simple covalent compounds form individual molecules
Ionic compounds have weak bonds; simple covalent compounds have strong bonds
There is no structural difference
questions.answer: Ionic compounds form giant lattices of ions; simple covalent compounds form individual molecules

questions.question: Summarise the bonding in sodium carbonate (Na₂CO₃) and explain how its properties relate to its bonding.
options: Only ionic bonding; high melting point due to strong ionic bonds
Only covalent bonding; low melting point due to weak intermolecular forces
Covalent bonding within CO₃²⁻ ions, ionic bonding between Na⁺ and CO₃²⁻; high melting point due to strong electrostatic forces between ions; conducts when molten or dissolved due to mobile ions
Weak intermolecular forces between Na₂CO₃ molecules; low melting point
questions.answer: Covalent bonding within CO₃²⁻ ions, ionic bonding between Na⁺ and CO₃²⁻; high melting point due to strong electrostatic forces between ions; conducts when molten or dissolved due to mobile ions

questions.question: Substance Z has the formula Ca(OH)₂. Identify all types of bonding and predict its properties.
options: Only ionic bonding; low melting point; does not conduct
Only covalent bonding; low melting point; does not conduct in any state
Covalent bonding within OH⁻ ions; ionic bonding between Ca²⁺ and OH⁻; high melting point; conducts when molten or dissolved
Metallic bonding; conducts in solid state
questions.answer: Covalent bonding within OH⁻ ions; ionic bonding between Ca²⁺ and OH⁻; high melting point; conducts when molten or dissolved

questions.question:
Compare the following substances:

A: NH₃ (ammonia)
B: NH₄Cl (ammonium chloride)

Which statement correctly describes their similarities and differences?
options: Both are ionic compounds with similar properties
Both are covalent compounds with similar properties
NH₃ is a simple covalent molecule (low MP, doesn't conduct); NH₄Cl contains the NH₄⁺ ion (covalent bonding within) bonded ionically to Cl⁻ (high MP, conducts when molten)
NH₃ is ionic; NH₄Cl is covalent
questions.answer: NH₃ is a simple covalent molecule (low MP, doesn't conduct); NH₄Cl contains the NH₄⁺ ion (covalent bonding within) bonded ionically to Cl⁻ (high MP, conducts when molten)

questions.question:
A chemist has three white solids:

1. Potassium sulfate (K₂SO₄)
2. Naphthalene (C₁₀H₈) - a simple covalent compound
3. Ammonium nitrate (NH₄NO₃)

Which would conduct electricity when dissolved in water?
options: Only compound 1
Only compound 2
Compounds 1 and 3 (both are ionic and dissociate into ions in water)
All three compounds
questions.answer: Compounds 1 and 3 (both are ionic and dissociate into ions in water)

questions.question: Explain why calcium oxide (CaO) has a very high melting point (2613°C) compared to carbon monoxide (CO) which has a very low melting point (-205°C).
options: Both are covalent but CaO has stronger bonds

CaO is an ionic compound with strong electrostatic forces between Ca²⁺ and O²⁻ ions requiring large amounts of energy to overcome; CO is a simple covalent molecule with weak intermolecular forces requiring little energy to overcome
Both are ionic but CO has smaller ions
CaO is covalent; CO is ionic
questions.answer: CaO is an ionic compound with strong electrostatic forces between Ca²⁺ and O²⁻ ions requiring large amounts of energy to overcome; CO is a simple covalent molecule with weak intermolecular forces requiring little energy to overcome

questions.question:
A student investigates four substances and records their properties:

Substance W: High MP, conducts when molten, does not conduct as solid
Substance X: Low MP, does not conduct in any state
Substance Y: High MP, conducts when dissolved in water, contains polyatomic ions
Substance Z: Very low MP, gas at room temperature, does not conduct

Which substances are ionic?
options: Only W
W and Y
X and Z
All four substances
questions.answer: W and Y

questions.question:
A student is asked to explain why:

(a) Sodium chloride conducts electricity when molten but not when solid
(b) Methane does not conduct electricity in any state
(c) Sodium sulfate conducts electricity when dissolved in water

Which explanation is correct for all three?
options: (a) Electrons become mobile when NaCl melts; (b) Methane has no electrons; (c) Water provides electrons
(a) Na⁺ and Cl⁻ ions become mobile when molten; (b) Methane is a simple covalent molecule with no ions or free electrons; (c) Na⁺ and SO₄²⁻ ions dissociate and become mobile in solution
(a) Covalent bonds break when melted; (b) Methane is ionic; (c) Sodium sulfate is covalent
(a) New ions form when melted; (b) Methane molecules cannot move; (c) Chemical reaction with water creates ions
questions.answer: (a) Na⁺ and Cl⁻ ions become mobile when molten; (b) Methane is a simple covalent molecule with no ions or free electrons; (c) Na⁺ and SO₄²⁻ ions dissociate and become mobile in solution
